content_of_post_to_comment:
Everyone about to sit a 10 day course gets the same prep advice: build up to an hour of sitting a day beforehand, get your knees used to it, read ahead. I did all of it before my first course at Dhammamanda in NorCal. It bought me almost nothing. Six courses at three centers later and I still can't point to one sit I did in advance that made day 3 easier.

The only prep that has ever worked for me isn't physical. It's making one decision before you arrive: you are not leaving before day 4, whatever argument shows up. And an argument will show up. Somewhere in days 2 to 4 your mind builds a genuinely persuasive case for going home. Work stuff. A real family reason. A strain in your back that feels less like pain and more like a signal. None of it is rational, it just wears the costume convincingly.

Pre-deciding beats it because you cannot win an argument with your own mind while you're sitting inside the discomfort that's generating it. The discipline in there was never about being strong in the moment. It was about having removed the option earlier, back when I was calm and the decision cost nothing.

Not a teacher, six courses in and still clumsy at this. But the people who left, every single course, were the ones who had kept leaving as an open question. written with ai
comment: The part about removing the option beforehand feels very true beyond retreats too. A lot of decisions get harder when we're already tired, scared, or uncomfortable because the mind starts negotiating from a defensive place. I wonder if the same approach changes after day 4 though, when staying stops being a rule and starts becoming an actual choice.
